revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kozuka]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: Programmatically generating so that if this gets imported to wikipedia as a draft, the bot will attribute it to Immanuelle and send them g6 warnings
revision.text: #redirect[[en:Kozuka]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle

revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[農工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[...'
revision.text:
'''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[農工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[ナイフ]]）に通じる。長さ15～30cm程度。
鉄製農工具は、農機具のほか加工に用いられる[[工具]]と[[鉄器]]生産に用いられる鉄器生産関連工具に分類されるが、刀子はいずれにも帰属しない万能工具に分類される。一般的には加工工具として用いられるが、厨房具や[[化粧道具]]、[[儀仗]]用や[[木簡]]の表面を削る書刀としての用途もあった。
中国では[[殷]]代前期の[[二里頭文化]]から青銅製刀子がみられる。一般的には環頭形で、刃は真直なものから内湾するものまで様々で、墓の[[副葬品]]としても出土する。戦国時代には鉄製刀子が出現し、漢代には[[象嵌]]で装飾が施されたものや鞘口近くの環に[[紐]]を通したものも見られる。
大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。日本では石製の模造品があり、[[古墳時代]]には[鉄器]が普及し、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土し、特に[[京都府]][[相楽郡]][[山代町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、後期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
[[Category:考古資料|とうす]]

大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。日本では石製の模造品があり、[[古墳時代]]には[[鉄器]]が普及し、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土し、特に[[京都府]][[相楽郡]][[山代町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、後期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
[[Category:考古資料|とうす]]

[[Category:考古資料|とうす]]

'''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[農工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[ナイフ]]）に通じる。長さ15～30cm程度。
鉄製農工具は、農機具のほか加工に用いられる[[工具]]と[[鉄器]]生産に用いられる鉄器生産関連工具に分類されるが、刀子はいずれにも帰属しない万能工具に分類される。一般的には加工工具として用いられるが、厨房具や[[化粧道具]]、[[儀仗]]用や[[木簡]]の表面を削る書刀としての用途もあった。
中国では[[殷]]代前期の[[二里頭文化]]から青銅製刀子がみられる。一般的には環頭形で、刃は真直なものから内湾するものまで様々で、墓の[[副葬品]]としても出土する。戦国時代には鉄製刀子が出現し、漢代には[[象嵌]]で装飾が施されたものや鞘口近くの環に[[紐]]を通したものも見られる。
大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。日本では石製の模造品があり、[[古墳時代]]には[[鉄器]]が普及し、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土し、特に[[京都府]][[相楽郡]][[山城町 (京都府)|山城町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、後期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
一般に刀剣類は武人・[[武官]]を象徴する物というイメージが強いが、古代の東洋においては刀子は[[文人]]・[[文官]]を象徴する物である。それは[[紙]]が貴重品で[[木簡]]・[[竹簡]]が広く使用されていた時代において、書き間違えた文字を削って修正するために必須の物だったからである。[[正倉院]]御物に刀子が現存しているが、これは[[武器]]ではなく[[文房具]]に分類される。
{{DEFAULTSORT:とうす}} {{stub}} [[Category:刀剣]] [[Category:文房具]] [[Category:考古資料|とうす]]

'''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[ナイフ]]）に通じる。長さ15～30cm程度。
鉄製農工具は、農機具のほか加工に用いられる[[工具]]と[[鉄器]]生産に用いられる鉄器生産関連工具に分類されるが、刀子はいずれにも帰属しない万能工具に分類される。一般的には加工工具として用いられるが、厨房具や[[化粧]]道具、[[儀仗]]用や[[木簡]]の表面を削る書刀としての用途もあった。
中国では[[殷]]代前期の[[二里頭文化]]から青銅製刀子がみられる。一般的には環頭形で、刃は真直なものから内湾するものまで様々で、墓の[[副葬品]]としても出土する。戦国時代には鉄製刀子が出現し、漢代には[[象嵌]]で装飾が施されたものや鞘口近くの環に[[紐]]を通したものも見られる。
大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。日本では石製の模造品があり、[[古墳時代]]には[[鉄器]]が普及し、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土し、特に[[京都府]][[木津川市]][[山城町 (京都府)|山城町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、後期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
一般に刀剣類は武人・[[武官]]を象徴するものというイメージが強いが、古代の東洋においては刀子は[[文人]]・[[文官]]を象徴するものである。それは[[紙]]が貴重品で[[木簡]]・[[竹簡]]が広く使用されていた時代において、書き間違えた文字を削って修正するために必須のものだったからである。[[正倉院]]宝物に刀子が現存しているが、これは[[武器]]ではなく[[文房具]]に分類される。
{{history-stub}} {{weapon-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:とうす}} [[Category:刀剣]] [[Category:文房具]] [[Category:考古資料]] [[Category:日本刀]]

'''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[ナイフ]]）に通じる。長さ15～30cm程度。 == 概要 == 鉄製農工具は、農機具のほか加工に用いられる[[工具]]と[[鉄器]]生産に用いられる鉄器生産関連工具に分類されるが、刀子はいずれにも帰属しない万能工具に分類される。一般的には加工工具として用いられるが、厨房具や[[化粧]]道具、[[儀仗]]用や[[木簡]]の表面を削る書刀としての用途もあった。
中国では[[殷]]代前期の[[二里頭文化]]から青銅製刀子がみられる。一般的には環頭形で、刃は真直なものから内湾するものまで様々で、墓の[[副葬品]]としても出土する。[[戦国時代 (中国)|戦国時代]]には鉄製刀子が出現し、漢代には[[象嵌]]で装飾が施されたものや鞘口近くの環に[[紐]]を通したものも見られる。
大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。
== 日本 == 三崎山遺跡（山形県飽海郡遊佐町）では大陸との交易によって入手したとみられる約3000年前の青銅刀子が出土している<ref>横山昭男・誉田慶信・伊藤清郎・渡辺信『山形県の歴史』p.21-22</ref>。縄文時代後・晩期には青銅製刀子の模造品とみられる<ref>日本の考古学Ⅱ 縄文時代 鎌木義昌編 1965年 河出書房 pp.430-431</ref>[[石剣|石刀]]の出土が見られ、[[古墳時代]]には[[鉄器]]が普及の普及が始まり、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土する。特に[[京都府]][[木津川市]][[山城町 (京都府)|山城町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、後期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
一般に刀剣類は武人・[[武官]]を象徴するものというイメージが強いが、古代の東洋においては刀子は[[文人]]・[[文官]]を象徴するものである。それは[[紙]]が貴重品で[[木簡]]・[[竹簡]]が広く使用されていた時代において、書き間違えた文字を削って修正するために必須のものだったからである。[[正倉院]]宝物に刀子が現存しているが、これは[[武器]]ではなく[[文房具]]に分類される。

== 日本 == 三崎山遺跡（山形県飽海郡遊佐町）では大陸との交易によって入手したとみられる約3000年前の青銅刀子が出土している<ref>横山昭男・誉田慶信・伊藤清郎・渡辺信『山形県の歴史』p.21-22</ref>。縄文時代後・晩期には青銅製刀子の模造品とみられる<ref>日本の考古学II 縄文時代 鎌木義昌編 1965年 河出書房 pp.430-431</ref>[[石剣|石刀]]の出土が見られ、[[古墳時代]]には[[鉄器]]の普及が始まり、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土する。特に[[京都府]][[木津川市]][[山城町 (京都府)|山城町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、前期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
一般に刀剣類は武人・[[武官]]を象徴するものというイメージが強いが<ref>事実、日本の律令時代では兵が装備するものの一つとして、「刀子1つ」が定められており、兵装の一つとして認識されていた。参考･『日本思想体系 律令』 [[岩波書店]] 1976年</ref>、古代の東洋においては刀子は[[文人]]・[[文官]]を象徴するものである。それは[[紙]]が貴重品で[[木簡]]・[[竹簡]]が広く使用されていた時代において、書き間違えた文字を削って修正するために必須のものだったからである。[[正倉院]]宝物に刀子が現存しているが、これは[[武器]]ではなく[[文房具]]に分類される。

== 参考文献 ==
== 関連項目 == *[[鉄刀]]
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:とうす}} [[Category:刀剣]] [[Category:文房具]] [[Category:考古資料]] [[Category:日本刀]]

'''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[ナイフ]]）に通じる。長さ15〜30cm程度。

大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[tool]] used for cutting, shaving, or other forms of [[processing]]. It is similar to the modern [[small knife]] (a compact multi-purpose [[knife]]), typically 15-30 cm in length. == Overview == Iron agricultural tools are categorized as either farming implements, processing tools, or tools related to [[ironware]] production. Tōsu falls into the category of multi-purpose tools that do not belong to either of the other groups. While ge..."
revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[tool]] used for cutting, shaving, or other forms of [[processing]]. It is similar to the modern [[small knife]] (a compact multi-purpose [[knife]]), typically 15-30 cm in length.
== Overview == Iron agricultural tools are categorized as either farming implements, processing tools, or tools related to [[ironware]] production. Tōsu falls into the category of multi-purpose tools that do not belong to either of the other groups. While generally used as a processing tool, tōsu also served as a kitchen utensil, [[cosmetic]] accessory, [[ceremonial weapon]] for rituals, or a writing knife to shave the surface of [[wooden tablets]].
In [[China]], bronze tōsu have been found dating back to the early [[Erlitou culture]] during the [[Yin]] dynasty. These were generally ring-handled, with blades ranging from straight to curved. They have also been excavated as [[funerary goods]] from tombs. By the [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear, and in the [[Han dynasty]], some tōsu featured [[inlays]] for decoration or rings near the scabbard mouth for attaching [[cords]].
Continental tōsu were also found in [[Korea]] and [[Japan]] as imported items.
== Japan == At the Misakiyama archaeological site in [[Yuza]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], a bronze tōsu from approximately 3,000 years ago—believed to have been acquired through trade with the continent—was excavated.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Honda Yoshinobu, Ito Seiro, Watanabe Makoto, *History of Yamagata Prefecture*, pp. 21-22</ref> During the late [[Jomon period]], imitations of bronze tōsu, such as [[stone swords]], have been found.<ref>*Japanese Archaeology II: Jomon Period*, Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobo, 1965, pp. 430-431</ref> In the [[Kofun period]], [[ironware]] became widespread, and iron tōsu began appearing as [[grave goods]] in tombs. Numerous examples have been excavated from early kofun sites, such as the [[Tsubai Otsukayama Kofun]] in [[Kizugawa]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. By the [[6th century]], tōsu became more common for everyday use, with variations featuring [[gilt bronze]] decorations, fitted [[sheaths]], and patterned handles.
Although [[bladed weapons]] like swords are often associated with [[warriors]] and [[military officers]],<ref>In fact, during Japan’s ritsuryō era, tōsu were listed as one of the required items for soldiers, categorizing them as part of military equipment. See *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized [[scholars]] and [[civil officials]]. This is because they were indispensable for correcting errors on [[wooden tablets]] or [[bamboo slips]] in an era when [[paper]] was a precious commodity. Several tōsu from the [[Shosoin]] treasures survive today, classified not as [[weapons]] but as [[stationery]].
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Bibliography == * *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1976.
== See Also == * [[Iron sword]] * [[Makiri]] * [[Puukko]]
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} [[Category:Bladed weapons]] [[Category:Stationery]] [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Japanese swords]]

See *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized [[scholars]] and [[civil officials]]. == Japan == At the Misakiyama archaeological site in [[Yuza]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], a bronze tōsu from approximately 3,000 years ago—believed to have been acquired through trade with the continent—was excavated.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Honda Yoshinobu, Ito Seiro, Watanabe Makoto, *History of Yamagata Prefecture*, pp. 21-22</ref> During the late [[Jomon period]], imitations of bronze tōsu, such as [[stone swords]], have been found.<ref>*Japanese Archaeology II: Jomon Period*, Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobo, 1965, pp. 430-431</ref> In the [[Kofun period]], [[ironware]] became widespread, and iron tōsu began appearing as [[grave goods]] in tombs. By the [[6th century]], tōsu became more common for everyday use, with variations featuring [[gilt bronze]] decorations, fitted [[sheaths]], and patterned handles.
Although [[bladed weapons]] like swords are often associated with [[warriors]] and [[military officers]],<ref>In fact, during Japan’s ritsuryō era, tōsu were listed as one of the required items for soldiers, categorizing them as part of military equipment. See *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized [[scholars]] and [[civil officials]]. {{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} [[Category:Bladed weapons]] [[Category:Stationery]] [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Japanese swords]] [[Category:Kk]][[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[category:original pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:newer pages]][[Category:newer pages]][[ja:刀子]]

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[tool]] used for cutting, shaving, or other forms of [[processing]]. It is similar to the modern [[small knife]] (a compact multi-purpose [[knife]]), typically 15-30 cm in length.
== Overview == Iron agricultural tools are categorized as either farming implements, processing tools, or tools related to [[ironware]] production. Tōsu falls into the category of multi-purpose tools that do not belong to either of the other groups. While generally used as a processing tool, tōsu also served as a kitchen utensil, [[cosmetic]] accessory, [[ceremonial weapon]] for rituals, or a writing knife to shave the surface of [[wooden tablets]].
In [[China]], bronze tōsu have been found dating back to the early [[Erlitou culture]] during the [[Yin]] dynasty. These were generally ring-handled, with blades ranging from straight to curved. They have also been excavated as [[funerary goods]] from tombs. By the [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear, and in the [[Han dynasty]], some tōsu featured [[inlays]] for decoration or rings near the scabbard mouth for attaching [[cords]].
Continental tōsu were also found in [[Korea]] and [[Japan]] as imported items.
== Japan == At the Misakiyama archaeological site in [[Yuza]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], a bronze tōsu from approximately 3,000 years ago—believed to have been acquired through trade with the continent—was excavated.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Honda Yoshinobu, Ito Seiro, Watanabe Makoto, *History of Yamagata Prefecture*, pp. 21-22</ref> During the late [[Jomon period]], imitations of bronze tōsu, such as [[stone swords]], have been found.<ref>*Japanese Archaeology II: Jomon Period*, Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobo, 1965, pp. 430-431</ref> In the [[Kofun period]], [[ironware]] became widespread, and iron tōsu began appearing as [[grave goods]] in tombs. Numerous examples have been excavated from early kofun sites, such as the [[Tsubai Otsukayama Kofun]] in [[Kizugawa]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. By the [[6th century]], tōsu became more common for everyday use, with variations featuring [[gilt bronze]] decorations, fitted [[sheaths]], and patterned handles.
Although [[bladed weapons]] like swords are often associated with [[warriors]] and [[military officers]],<ref>In fact, during Japan’s ritsuryō era, tōsu were listed as one of the required items for soldiers, categorizing them as part of military equipment. See *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized [[scholars]] and [[civil officials]]. This is because they were indispensable for correcting errors on [[wooden tablets]] or [[bamboo slips]] in an era when [[paper]] was a precious commodity. Several tōsu from the [[Shosoin]] treasures survive today, classified not as [[weapons]] but as [[stationery]].

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[tool]] used for cutting, shaving, or other forms of [[processing]]. It is similar to the modern [[small knife]] (a compact multi-purpose [[knife]]), typically 15-30 cm in length.
== Overview == Iron agricultural tools are categorized as either farming implements, processing tools, or tools related to [[ironware]] production. Tōsu falls into the category of multi-purpose tools that do not belong to either of the other groups. While generally used as a processing tool, tōsu also served as a kitchen utensil, [[cosmetic]] accessory, [[ceremonial weapon]] for rituals, or a writing knife to shave the surface of [[wooden tablets]].
In [[China]], bronze tōsu have been found dating back to the early [[Erlitou culture]] during the [[Yin]] dynasty. These were generally ring-handled, with blades ranging from straight to curved. They have also been excavated as [[funerary goods]] from tombs. By the [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear, and in the [[Han dynasty]], some tōsu featured [[inlays]] for decoration or rings near the scabbard mouth for attaching [[cords]].
Continental tōsu were also found in [[Korea]] and [[Japan]] as imported items.
== Japan == At the Misakiyama archaeological site in [[Yuza]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], a bronze tōsu from approximately 3,000 years ago—believed to have been acquired through trade with the continent—was excavated.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Honda Yoshinobu, Ito Seiro, Watanabe Makoto, *History of Yamagata Prefecture*, pp. 21-22</ref> During the late [[Jomon period]], imitations of bronze tōsu, such as [[stone swords]], have been found.<ref>*Japanese Archaeology II: Jomon Period*, Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobo, 1965, pp. 430-431</ref> In the [[Kofun period]], [[ironware]] became widespread, and iron tōsu began appearing as [[grave goods]] in tombs. Numerous examples have been excavated from early kofun sites, such as the [[Tsubai Otsukayama Kofun]] in [[Kizugawa]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. By the [[6th century]], tōsu became more common for everyday use, with variations featuring [[gilt bronze]] decorations, fitted [[sheaths]], and patterned handles.
Although [[bladed weapons]] like swords are often associated with [[warriors]] and [[military officers]],<ref>In fact, during Japan’s ritsuryō era, tōsu were listed as one of the required items for soldiers, categorizing them as part of military equipment. See *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized [[scholars]] and [[civil officials]]. This is because they were indispensable for correcting errors on [[wooden tablets]] or [[bamboo slips]] in an era when [[paper]] was a precious commodity. Several tōsu from the [[Shosoin]] treasures survive today, classified not as [[weapons]] but as [[stationery]].
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Bibliography == * *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1976.
== See Also == * [[Iron sword]] * [[Makiri]] * [[Puukko]]

revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[tool]] used for cutting, shaving, or other forms of [[processing]]. It is similar to the modern [[small knife]] (a compact multi-purpose [[knife]]), typically 15-30 cm in length.
== Overview == Iron agricultural tools are categorized as either farming implements, processing tools, or tools related to [[ironware]] production. Tōsu falls into the category of multi-purpose tools that do not belong to either of the other groups. While generally used as a processing tool, tōsu also served as a kitchen utensil, [[cosmetic]] accessory, [[ceremonial weapon]] for rituals, or a writing knife to shave the surface of [[wooden tablets]].
In [[China]], bronze tōsu have been found dating back to the early [[Erlitou culture]] during the [[Yin]] dynasty. These were generally ring-handled, with blades ranging from straight to curved. They have also been excavated as [[funerary goods]] from tombs. By the [[Warring States period (China)|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear, and in the [[Han dynasty]], some tōsu featured [[inlays]] for decoration or rings near the scabbard mouth for attaching [[cords]].
Continental tōsu were also found in [[Korea]] and [[Japan]] as imported items.
== Japan == At the Misakiyama archaeological site in [[Yuza]], [[Yamagata Prefecture]], a bronze tōsu from approximately 3,000 years ago—believed to have been acquired through trade with the continent—was excavated.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Honda Yoshinobu, Ito Seiro, Watanabe Makoto, *History of Yamagata Prefecture*, pp. 21-22</ref> During the late [[Jomon period]], imitations of bronze tōsu, such as [[stone swords]], have been found.<ref>*Japanese Archaeology II: Jomon Period*, Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobo, 1965, pp. 430-431</ref> In the [[Kofun period]], [[ironware]] became widespread, and iron tōsu began appearing as [[grave goods]] in tombs. Numerous examples have been excavated from early kofun sites, such as the [[Tsubai Otsukayama Kofun]] in [[Kizugawa]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]]. By the [[6th century]], tōsu became more common for everyday use, with variations featuring [[gilt bronze]] decorations, fitted [[sheaths]], and patterned handles.
Although [[bladed weapons]] like swords are often associated with [[warriors]] and [[military officers]],<ref>In fact, during Japan’s ritsuryō era, tōsu were listed as one of the required items for soldiers, categorizing them as part of military equipment. See *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized [[scholars]] and [[civil officials]]. This is because they were indispensable for correcting errors on [[wooden tablets]] or [[bamboo slips]] in an era when [[paper]] was a precious commodity. Several tōsu from the [[Shosoin]] treasures survive today, classified not as [[weapons]] but as [[stationery]].
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Bibliography == * *Japanese Thought Anthology: Ritsuryō*, [[Iwanami Shoten]], 1976.
== See Also == * [[Iron sword]] * [[Makiri]] * [[Puukko]]
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} [[Category:Bladed weapons]] [[Category:Stationery]] [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Japanese swords]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[category:original pages]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:dardar]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tōsu (tool)]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:刀子]]

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} [[Category:Bladed tools]] [[Category:Stationery]] [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Japanese swords]] [[ja:刀子]]

Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>

By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. '''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).

Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} [[Category:Bladed tools]] [[Category:Stationery]] [[Category:Archaeological artifacts]] [[Category:Japanese swords]] [[Category:Kozuka]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] <!--Kozuka--> [[Category:Kozuka]][[Category:Ko-dachi]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kozuka]][[Category:kk]]
[[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:刀子]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kozuka}}

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

[[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]] [[ja:刀子]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kozuka]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[en:Kozuka]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Kozuka}}[[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:11]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kozuka]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kozuka}} [[Category:New]]

== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>

Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.

'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具}}.
== References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ}}

=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=draft}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=draft}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=draft}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳|12=draft}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣|12=draft}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=draft}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡|12=draft}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=draft}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙|12=draft}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院|12=draft}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=draft}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=draft}}
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}}
