revision.text:
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|tool|ja|工具|12=draft}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=draft}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=draft}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=draft}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=draft}} accessory, a {{ill|ritual implement|ja|儀仗|12=draft}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡|12=draft}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|ja|二里頭文化|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|ja|殷|12=draft}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=draft}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|grave goods|ja|副葬品|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|ja|戦国時代 (中国)|12=draft}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=draft}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=draft}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|12=draft}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=draft}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|kofun|ja|古墳|1=Kofun|12=draft}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|ja|椿井大塚山古墳|12=draft}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|bladed weapons|ja|刀剣|12=draft}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=draft}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡|12=draft}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=draft}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙|12=draft}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|ja|正倉院|12=draft}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|stationery|ja|文房具|12=draft}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=draft}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=draft}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=draft}}
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}}

During periods when {{ill|mokkan|ja|木簡|12=draft}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=draft}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|paper|ja|紙|12=draft}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. revision.text:
'''刀子'''（とうす）は、ものを切る、削るなど[[加工]]の用途に用いられる[[工具]]の一種。現代の[[小刀]]（小型万能[[ナイフ]]）に通じる。長さ15 - 30cm程度。
== 概要 == 鉄製農工具は、農機具のほか加工に用いられる[[工具]]と[[鉄器]]生産に用いられる鉄器生産関連工具に分類されるが、刀子はいずれにも帰属しない万能工具に分類される。一般的には加工工具として用いられるが、厨房具や[[化粧]]道具、[[儀仗]]用や[[木簡]]の表面を削る書刀としての用途もあった。
中国では[[殷]]代前期の[[二里頭文化]]から青銅製刀子がみられる。一般的には環頭形で、刃は真直なものから内湾するものまで様々で、墓の[[副葬品]]としても出土する。[[戦国時代 (中国)|戦国時代]]には鉄製刀子が出現し、漢代には[[象嵌]]で装飾が施されたものや鞘口近くの環に[[紐]]を通したものも見られる。
大陸製の刀子は渡来品として日本や[[朝鮮半島]]でも見られる。
== 日本 == 三崎山遺跡（山形県飽海郡遊佐町）では大陸との交易によって入手したとみられる約3000年前の青銅刀子が出土している<ref>横山昭男・誉田慶信・伊藤清郎・渡辺信『山形県の歴史』p.21-22</ref>。縄文時代後・晩期には青銅製刀子の模造品とみられる<ref>日本の考古学II 縄文時代 鎌木義昌編 1965年 河出書房 pp.430-431</ref>[[石剣|石刀]]の出土が見られ、[[古墳時代]]には[[鉄器]]の普及が始まり、4世紀には刀子をふくめ[[古墳]]の[[副葬品]]として鉄器製品が出土する。特に[[京都府]][[木津川市]][[山城町 (京都府)|山城町]]の[[椿井大塚山古墳]]など、前期古墳からの出土例が多い。[[6世紀]]以降には一般においても使われるようになり、金銅で装飾が施されているものや、刀身に合わせた[[鞘]]に収められているもの、把手に文様をもつものなどが出現する。
一般に刀剣類は武人・[[武官]]を象徴するものというイメージが強いが<ref>事実、日本の律令時代では兵が装備するものの一つとして、「刀子1つ」が定められており、兵装の一つとして認識されていた。『日本思想大系 律令』 [[岩波書店]] 1976年</ref>、古代の東洋においては刀子は[[文人]]・[[文官]]を象徴するものである。それは[[紙]]が貴重品で[[木簡]]・[[竹簡]]が広く使用されていた時代において、書き間違えた文字を削って修正するために必須のものだったからである。[[正倉院]]宝物に刀子が現存しているが、これは[[武器]]ではなく[[文房具]]に分類される。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 参考文献 == *『[[日本思想大系]] 律令』 [[岩波書店]] 1976年 == 関連項目 == *[[鉄刀]] * [[マキリ]] * [[プーッコ]]
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:とうす}} [[Category:刀剣]] [[Category:文房具]] [[Category:考古資料]] [[Category:日本刀]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[刀子]] to [[Kozuka]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 参考文献 == *『[[日本思想大系]] 律令』 [[岩波書店]] 1976年 == 関連項目 == *[[鉄刀]] * [[マキリ]] * [[プーッコ]]
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}} {{デフォルトソート:とうす}} [[Category:刀剣]] [[Category:文房具]] [[Category:考古資料]] [[Category:日本刀]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11396050]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[:en:Hand tool|tool]] used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|qq=|1=kogatana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|qq=|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|qq=|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|qq=|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a [[:en:Ceremonial weapon|ritual implement]], or even as a knife for shaving the surface of [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early [[:en:Erlitou culture|Erlitou culture]] during the [[:en:Shang dynasty|Shang dynasty]] period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as [[:en:Grave goods|grave goods]]. During the [[:en:Warring States period|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|qq=|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|qq=|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].

In the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11396050]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[:en:Hand tool|tool]] used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|1=kogatana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a [[:en:Ceremonial weapon|ritual implement]], or even as a knife for shaving the surface of [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early [[:en:Erlitou culture|Erlitou culture]] during the [[:en:Shang dynasty|Shang dynasty]] period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as [[:en:Grave goods|grave goods]]. During the [[:en:Warring States period|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}

[[ja:刀子]]
<!--Kozuka--> [[Category:Swords]] [[Category:Stationery]] [[Category:Archaeological data]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11396050}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11396050]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[:en:Hand tool|tool]] used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|1=kogatana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a [[:en:Ceremonial weapon|ritual implement]], or even as a knife for shaving the surface of [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early [[:en:Erlitou culture|Erlitou culture]] during the [[:en:Shang dynasty|Shang dynasty]] period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as [[:en:Grave goods|grave goods]]. During the [[:en:Warring States period|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}

=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a [[:en:Ceremonial weapon|ritual implement]], or even as a knife for shaving the surface of [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early [[:en:Erlitou culture|Erlitou culture]] during the [[:en:Shang dynasty|Shang dynasty]] period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as [[:en:Grave goods|grave goods]]. During the [[:en:Warring States period|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}

By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11396050]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of [[:en:Hand tool|tool]] used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|1=kogatana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a [[:en:Ceremonial weapon|ritual implement]], or even as a knife for shaving the surface of [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early [[:en:Erlitou culture|Erlitou culture]] during the [[:en:Shang dynasty|Shang dynasty]] period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as [[:en:Grave goods|grave goods]]. During the [[:en:Warring States period|Warring States period]], iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]], imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]], the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] tombs, such as those found at the [[:en:Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun]] in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although [[:en:Sword|bladed weapons]] are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when [[:en:Mokkan|mokkan]] and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of [[:en:Paper|paper]], tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the [[:en:Shōsōin|Shōsōin]] are classified not as weapons but as [[:en:Stationery|stationery]].

== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}}

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11396050]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:41, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|Hand tool|en|Hand tool|WD=Q2578402|lt=tool}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|1=kogatana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a {{ill|Ceremonial weapon|en|Ceremonial weapon|WD=Q15097957|lt=ritual implement}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|en|Erlitou culture|WD=Q1051663|lt=Erlitou culture}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=grave goods}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|en|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|WD=Q11540994|lt=Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|Sword|en|Sword|WD=Q12791|lt=bladed weapons}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|Paper|en|Paper|WD=Q11472|lt=paper}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|Stationery|en|Stationery|WD=Q875696|lt=stationery}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}}

Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|Stationery|en|Stationery|WD=Q875696|lt=stationery}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}

revision.comment: adding dummy category to make categories work
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]]
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|Hand tool|en|Hand tool|WD=Q2578402|lt=tool}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|1=kogatana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=make-up|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a {{ill|Ceremonial weapon|en|Ceremonial weapon|WD=Q15097957|lt=ritual implement}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|en|Erlitou culture|WD=Q1051663|lt=Erlitou culture}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=grave goods}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|en|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|WD=Q11540994|lt=Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|Sword|en|Sword|WD=Q12791|lt=bladed weapons}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|Paper|en|Paper|WD=Q11472|lt=paper}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|Stationery|en|Stationery|WD=Q875696|lt=stationery}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}
{{history-stub}} {{Product-stub}}

Although {{ill|Sword|en|Sword|WD=Q12791|lt=bladed weapons|1=Sword}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan|1=Mokkan}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=Bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|Paper|en|Paper|WD=Q11472|lt=paper|1=Paper}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|Stationery|en|Stationery|WD=Q875696|lt=stationery|1=Stationery}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:刀子]]
'''Tōsu''' (刀子) is a type of {{ill|Hand tool|en|Hand tool|WD=Q2578402|lt=tool|1=Hand tool}} used for cutting and shaving, among other tasks. It is similar to the modern {{ill|kogatana|ja|小刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kogatana|1=knife|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97169605}} (small utility knife). Its length typically ranges between 15 and 30 cm.
== Overview ==
=== General Use === Iron tools are generally classified as {{ill|agricultural implements|ja|農機具|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/agricultural implements|1=Agricultural implements|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97378908}}, tools for crafting, or implements related to the production of {{ill|ironware|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ironware|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}}. However, the tōsu does not fit neatly into these categories and is instead considered a multi-purpose tool. While commonly used as a crafting implement, it also served as a kitchen tool, a {{ill|cosmetic|ja|化粧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cosmetic|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Makeup|WD=Q1190910}} accessory, a {{ill|Ceremonial weapon|en|Ceremonial weapon|WD=Q15097957|lt=ritual implement|1=Ceremonial weapon}}, or even as a knife for shaving the surface of {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan|1=Mokkan}} (wooden tablets used for writing).
=== In China === In China, bronze tōsu have been discovered from the early {{ill|Erlitou culture|en|Erlitou culture|WD=Q1051663|lt=Erlitou culture|1=Erlitou culture}} during the {{ill|Shang dynasty|en|Shang dynasty|WD=Q128938|lt=Shang dynasty|1=Shang dynasty}} period. These tōsu were typically {{ill|ring-headed|ja|環頭形|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ring-headed|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and featured various blade shapes, ranging from straight to inward-curving edges. They were often included as {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=grave goods|1=Grave goods}}. During the {{ill|Warring States period|en|Warring States period|WD=Q185063|lt=Warring States period|1=Warring States period}}, iron tōsu began to appear. By the Han dynasty, tōsu with inlays, decorative designs, and attached rings for threading cords became common.
=== In Korea and Japan === Continental tōsu also made their way to Korea and Japan as imported items.
== In Japan ==
Bronze tōsu, likely acquired through trade with the mainland, were unearthed at the {{ill|Misakiyama site|ja|三崎山遺跡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Misakiyama site|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in Yusa Town, Yamagata Prefecture, dating back approximately 3,000 years.<ref>Yokoyama Akio, Kondō Keishin, Itō Kiyoshi, and Watanabe Shinobu, "The History of Yamagata Prefecture," pp. 21-22.</ref> In the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}}, imitation bronze tōsu made of {{ill|stone|ja|石剣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 石刀・石剣|1=Q11585192|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11585192}} have been discovered.<ref>"Japanese Archaeology II: The Jōmon Period," edited by Kamaki Yoshimasa, Kawade Shobō, 1965, pp. 430-431.</ref>
In the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}}, the introduction of {{ill|iron tools|ja|鉄器|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/iron tools|1=iron tool|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11649539}} began. By the 4th century, tōsu were included as grave goods in {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} tombs, such as those found at the {{ill|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|en|Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|WD=Q11540994|lt=Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun|1=Tsubai Ōtsukayama Kofun}} in Kyoto Prefecture. By the 6th century, tōsu became widely used, with some adorned with gold-bronze decorations or stored in scabbards matching the blade. Handles were sometimes embellished with patterns.
Although {{ill|Sword|en|Sword|WD=Q12791|lt=bladed weapons|1=Sword}} are commonly associated with warriors and military officials,<ref>"Nihon Shisō Taikei: Ritsuryō," Iwanami Shoten, 1976.</ref> in ancient East Asia, tōsu symbolized {{ill|literati|ja|文人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/literati|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 文人 (曖昧さ回避)|1=Literati|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1857552}} and civil officials. During periods when {{ill|Mokkan|en|Mokkan|WD=Q4299994|lt=mokkan|1=Mokkan}} and {{ill|bamboo slips|ja|竹簡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bamboo slips|1=Bamboo slips|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126510729}} were widely used due to the rarity of {{ill|Paper|en|Paper|WD=Q11472|lt=paper|1=Paper}}, tōsu were essential for correcting mistakes by shaving off errors. Tōsu preserved in the {{ill|Shōsōin|en|Shōsōin|WD=Q2379007|lt=Shōsōin|1=Shōsōin}} are classified not as weapons but as {{ill|Stationery|en|Stationery|WD=Q875696|lt=stationery|1=Stationery}}.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Iron swords|ja|鉄刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iron swords|1=iron sword|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q48750050}} * {{ill|Makiri|ja|マキリ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Makiri|1=makiri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17221886}} * {{ill|Puukko|ja|プーッコ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Puukko|1=Tollekniv|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1527860}}
